critic_raw:
This experiment predicting depression severity (PHQ-9) from EHR data using a fine-tuned clinical transformer versus ridge regression demonstrates rigorous attention to temporal splitting, preprocessing integrity, and confound documentation. The experimental design is generally sound, but several issues warrant critical examination.

The patient-level assignment to splits is explicitly described as ensuring 'all records for a single patient are assigned to a single split.' This is correct in principle, but with a 5-year longitudinal dataset and a temporal split, the implementation requires careful handling: patients who had encounters across multiple temporal periods (e.g., years 1-3 and year 4) must be assigned to one window, yet their encounters in the non-assigned window are lost. This can create substantial sample size reduction compared to the stated split ratios and introduces selection bias toward patients with concentrated encounter timing, which is not discussed.

The transformer model takes 'all encounter notes in the 12 months prior to the prediction date,' but clinical notes 12 months before a PHQ-9 assessment may contain direct references to prior depression severity assessments or treatment responses that constitute near-perfect label leakage. If prior PHQ-9 scores or phrases like 'patient reports improved mood since last visit' appear in notes used as inputs, the transformer has access to earlier versions of the label, which is a form of temporal target leakage distinct from the data split design.

The secondary ablation (transformer without notes) is described as isolating architectural effects, but the comparison is between ridge regression on a small hand-engineered feature set and a fine-tuned transformer on both structured features and notes. Isolating architectural effects would require a transformer trained on the same structured features as ridge regression—the ablation as described conflates note access with transformer architecture.

Statistical significance testing for the primary RMSE comparison is not mentioned in the experiment design. Given the test set spans only 12 months of a single health system, the number of independent patients in the test set may not support reliable significance claims, and the omission of confidence intervals or significance tests for the primary outcome is a notable gap.
all_issues_raised: Patient-level temporal assignment can cause significant sample loss and selection bias toward patients with encounter-concentrated timing, not discussed
Clinical notes from 12 months prior to prediction may contain explicit or implicit references to prior depression severity, constituting temporal target leakage
Ablation comparing transformer without notes does not isolate architecture from feature access; a transformer on structured features only is needed
No statistical significance testing or confidence intervals on primary RMSE comparison; test set may lack statistical power with 12 months of single-site data
Label definition (most recent PHQ-9 within 30 days of a clinical encounter) may introduce selection bias toward patients with scheduled follow-up, who differ systematically from those without
Temporal misalignment between clinical note features and PHQ-9 label: patients with infrequent clinical contact have labels less contemporaneous with observed features
End-to-end fine-tuning of large pre-trained transformer on ~30,000 training records without layer freezing strategy risks memorization over generalization
Baseline excludes prior PHQ-9 scores — the strongest single predictor of current severity — artificially handicapping the statistical baseline
Bootstrap confidence intervals on test set with repeated patients violate i.i.d. assumption; patient-level block bootstrap is required
No analysis of PHQ-9 documentation frequency variation across the patient population and how documentation gaps affect label quality and prediction reliability
Patient notes spanning the 12-month lookback window near split boundaries may cross temporal split thresholds, creating subtle data leakage for patients near train/validation/test boundaries
No minimally clinically important difference (MCID) defined for PHQ-9 RMSE, making it unclear whether statistically significant improvements correspond to clinically meaningful gains

Full fine-tuning of BioBERT (110M+ parameters) on a small single-institution dataset risks overfitting; standard large-model fine-tuning practices (layer freezing, gradual unfreezing) are not described
Ablation (transformer without notes) evaluated on test set constitutes a second hypothesis test, inflating familywise error rate without correction

No assessment of whether PHQ-9 scores are uniformly distributed across the 5-year period or whether documentation practices changed, which could produce distribution shift the temporal split conflates with model improvement
